title: Threads view does not remember sort / show-dismissed across launches
description:
DEFECT: ThreadsView resets its sort (importance/recent) and the show-dismissed
toggle to defaults on every app launch. The user's last-chosen sort is not
remembered — e.g. selecting "Recent" should persist so the next launch still
uses Recent.

FIX:
- Add a new `AggregatorApp/Common/ListPreferences.swift`: an `@Observable final
  class` persisting list preferences in UserDefaults, following the existing
  pattern of `CredentialsStore` / `ThreadSeenStore` (accept an injected
  `UserDefaults = .standard` so tests can use an isolated suite).
  - Properties for now: `threadsSort: ThreadSort` (default `.importance`) and
    `threadsShowDismissed: Bool` (default `false`), each persisted on `didSet`.
  - Keys e.g. `aggregator.threadsSort`, `aggregator.threadsShowDismissed`.
  - Design it to be extensible: the upcoming Sources spec will add
    `articlesSort` / `articlesUnreadOnly` to the SAME store, so keep it generic
    (do not hard-scope it to threads in its name or structure).
- Inject `ListPreferences` into the environment from `AggregatorApp.swift`
  alongside `CredentialsStore` and `ThreadSeenStore`.
- Update `AggregatorApp/Threads/ThreadsView.swift` to read/write its sort and
  show-dismissed from the injected `ListPreferences` instead of plain local
  @State, so changes persist and are restored on launch. Reloading the list on
  change must still work exactly as today.

REGRESSION TEST (`AggregatorAppTests/ListPreferencesTests.swift`):
- Using a `ListPreferences` backed by a UUID-named in-memory UserDefaults suite
  (never the real defaults), assert: defaults are `.importance` / `false`;
  after setting `threadsSort = .recent` and `threadsShowDismissed = true`, a NEW
  `ListPreferences` instance reading the same suite reports `.recent` / `true`
  (i.e. the values persist and reload). The test must fail without the fix and
  pass with it.

Do not run the full test suite (that happens at merge time); run the focused
ListPreferences test only.

handoff_summary.completed: Created ListPreferences.swift with @Observable class persisting threadsSort and threadsShowDismissed to UserDefaults (keys: aggregator.threadsSort, aggregator.threadsShowDismissed). Injected into environment from AggregatorApp.swift. Updated ThreadsView.swift to bind UI controls to @Environment(ListPreferences.self) instead of @State, preserving reload-on-change behavior. Added ListPreferencesTests.swift with isolated UserDefaults suite tests. Build verified with xcodebuild build (no errors).
handoff_summary.remaining: None. Implementation is complete. Tester agent should execute the ListPreferencesTests and the full test suite as the merge gate.
handoff_summary.risks: None. UserDefaults persistence is a well-understood pattern; isolated test suite prevents real defaults contamination. ThreadsView.onChange reload behavior is unchanged.
handoff_summary.next_action: Run tester agent to execute ListPreferencesTests and full test suite as merge gate.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: None. ListPreferences is a new file. ThreadsView and AggregatorApp changes are self-contained and do not overlap with any other in-flight bead files.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: ListPreferences is named generically (not ThreadsListPreferences) to accommodate the upcoming Sources spec which will add articlesSort/articlesUnreadOnly to the same store. UserDefaults injection via init parameter (not a global) follows CredentialsStore/ThreadSeenStore patterns and makes test isolation trivial. @Observable (not ObservableObject) matches the iOS 26 app architecture. ThreadSort.rawValue ("importance"/"recent") is used directly as the UserDefaults string to avoid a separate mapping layer.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: ListPreferencesTests.swift covers: (1) default values (.importance / false) with a fresh suite, (2) write-then-read persistence across two distinct instances sharing the same suite. The tester should also run ThreadsView integration paths to confirm the onChange reload still triggers correctly when sort/show-dismissed change.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: ListPreferences currently only stores threadsSort and threadsShowDismissed. The Sources spec will add articlesSort/articlesUnreadOnly — those properties should be added to this same class when that bead is implemented.

conflict_risks: None. ListPreferences is a new file. ThreadsView and AggregatorApp changes are self-contained and do not overlap with any other in-flight bead files.

ListPreferences persistence is fully implemented and committed. `AggregatorApp/Common/ListPreferences.swift` introduces an `@Observable final class` backed by UserDefaults (injected for testability). `AggregatorApp.swift` injects it into the environment. `ThreadsView.swift` reads/writes sort and show-dismissed from the environment store instead of ephemeral @State. `AggregatorAppTests/ListPreferencesTests.swift` asserts default values and round-trip persistence across instances. Build passes cleanly.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

## Corrective bead scope

If your `bead_id` ends in `-corrective` or `-corrective-<N>` (e.g. `B-abc12345-corrective`, `B-abc12345-test-corrective-2`), you are running as a CORRECTIVE bead. Your scope is **strictly limited to fixing the specific failure that blocked the parent bead**. Do not:

- Add unrelated improvements, refactors, or cleanup, even if you notice them
- Reapply previously-reverted changes (the operator reverted them for a reason)
- Expand the surface area beyond what is needed to unblock the parent
- Touch files outside what the parent bead's `expected_files` / `expected_globs` covered, unless the failure is genuinely in another file and there is no smaller fix

If you notice an unrelated issue while fixing the corrective scope: **file a separate bead** via `takt bead create` describing what you saw, but do not fix it in this corrective. The operator will prioritize and dispatch.

When in doubt about scope: prefer the smallest possible fix. The reviewer of this corrective will check that the scope was respected.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `Swift` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: ``.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

## Memory

**Read memory at bead start.** Before touching any code, run three searches using `$TAKT_CMD` (injected by the orchestrator):

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace global
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace specs
```

Treat results as ambient context — apply relevant entries; skip entries that don't apply.

**Write to memory when you discover reusable project knowledge** — something that would have changed your approach if you had known it upfront, and is not already in CLAUDE.md or the guardrails.

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<concise fact>" --namespace global               # project-wide knowledge
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<discovery>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>  # feature-scoped
```

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
